learning_outcomes.description: Apply game theoretic concepts and solution methods to analyse strategic interactions in economic contexts, demonstrating mathematical rigour and logical reasoning.
Determine and justify appropriate theoretical frameworks and equilibrium concepts to model economic scenarios involving strategic interactions.
Critically evaluate the assumptions, predictions, and limitations of game theoretic models when applied to real-world problems involving information asymmetries and strategic behaviour.
Analyse and critique research applications of game theory, assessing how theoretical models contribute to understanding strategic behaviour in economic contexts.
Effectively communicate economic research through oral presentations and written analysis, translating complex theoretical concepts into clear insights for diverse stakeholders.

assessments.task_description: An open book assignment involving problem solving and/or short answer questions. Questions can be given on any topic covered prior to the midsemester assignment. The task is designed to test the students' understanding of essential theoretical frameworks and to further their awareness of critical limitations of these frameworks and the scope of their applicability in a research context.
assessments.submission_guidelines: To be submitted via the course blackboard website.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

The task is designed to test the students' understanding of essential theoretical frameworks and to further their awareness of critical limitations of these frameworks and the scope of their applicability in a research context.

assessments.task_description:
Overview

This INDIVIDUAL presentation assignment requires students to critically analyse a chosen research article and communicate the analysis effectively to the class during a tutorial session.

Article Selection & Time Slot Reservation

  • Students must reserve both their chosen article AND presentation time slot on Blackboard on a first-come, first-served basis
  • Presentations will take place during scheduled tutorial sessions


Presentation Requirements

Your presentation must include the following three components:

Context & Related Literature

  • Identify and explain the research question addressed in the article
  • Discuss how this research fits within the literature
  • Explain the gap or need this research addresses

Analysis

  • Clearly highlight the article's main contribution(s)
  • Explain the significance of the findings or methodological / theoretical insights
  • Discuss the methodology or approach used

Future Research

  • Identify limitations and/or unanswered questions from the article
  • Suggest how future research could build upon or extend this work


Assessment Criteria

Students will be evaluated on:

  • Content Quality (60% weight) Accuracy and scope of analysis, critical evaluation, coverage of all three required components, demonstration of knowledge synthesis and evaluation through responses during the Q&A session related to all three components
  • Presentation Style (40% weight): clarity of communication, organisation, time management, effective use of visual aids, and ability to respond thoughtfully and confidently to audience questions


Presentation Format

  • Presentation duration: ca. 10 minutes
  • Slides are recommended but optional (ca. 3-5 content slides)
  • Q&A session to follow each presentation (ca. 5 minutes)
  • Presentations take place during the chosen tutorial session


Important Notes

  • Reserve both your article and time slot well in advance
  • Technical issues (computer problems, internet connectivity) will not be accepted as valid excuses
  • Attendance at your assigned tutorial session is mandatory
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
12/10/2026

The presentation event will be held in Week 11, with the exact date determined based on the availability of guests, students, and location. This will be communicated well in advance on blackboard. The presentation must be ready by Monday, Week 11.
assessments.task_description:
Overview

Students present their thesis research in front of an audience, for instance, invited guests from industry, academia, and/or the public sector. Each presentation should be approximately 10-15 minutes in duration and demonstrate both comprehensive understanding of the student's research and effective application of presentation techniques. Presentations should be accessible to a general academic audience without specialist knowledge of the topic.


The presentation should include and demonstrate:

  • Clear articulation of the research question, methodology, and key findings using language accessible to non-specialists
  • Demonstration of critical understanding of the research context and significance
  • Application of presentation techniques taught in the workshop sessions and developed as part of the earlier research article presentation


Evaluation

Students will be evaluated on factual accuracy, clarity of communication, organisation and structure, time management, effective use of visual aids, ability to effectively communicate their research to the audience, and more generally the demonstration of presentation skills developed throughout the semester.
